title: REPUBLIC OF KENYA
text:
REPUBLIC OF KENYA
IN THE TAX APPEALS TRIBUNAL AT NAIROBI
TAX APPEAL NUMBER E442 OF 2020
H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG...……..………..…...
APPELLANT
-VERSUS-
COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC
TAXES……………………....RESPONDENT
JUDGMENT
BACKGROUND
1. The Appellant H.P Gauff Ingenieure Gmbh & Co is a German
multinational with over 25 representations worldwide that helps to
carry out projects in the disciplines of water and sewerage,
transport, roads and rail, mobility and IT solutions, environment
and energy, urban planning and architecture.
2. The Respondent is a principal officer appointed under Section 13
of the Kenya Revenue Authority Act, CAP 469 of Kenya’s Laws
(hereinafter “the Act”). Under Section 5 (1) of the Act, the Kenya
Revenue Authority is an agency of the Government for the
collection and receipt of all tax revenue. Further, under Section
5(2) of the Act with respect to the performance of its functions
under subsection (1), the Authority is mandated to administer and
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 1 OF 34

enforce all provisions of the written laws as set out in Part 1 and 2
of the First Schedule to the Act for the purposes of assessing,
collecting and accounting for all revenues in accordance with
those laws.
3. The Respondent issued a notice of assessment on 25th March 2020
regarding corporation tax, PAYE and VAT totalling Kshs
1,955,787,203.00.
4. Appellant issued a notice of objection dated 28th April 2020. The
Respondent reviewed the objection based on the facts and
supporting documentation provided and issued an objection
decision dated 28th July 2020 wherein it demanded Kshs
1,955,787,204.00
5. The Appellant being aggrieved, filed notice of appeal dated 21st
August 2020.
6. The Tribunal heard and determined the matter vide a judgment
dated 2nd February 2024 wherein the Appeal was dismissed.
7. The Appellant being aggrieved by the decision, of the Tribunal filed
an appeal in the High Court Appeal No. E078 of 2024.
8. The High Court set aside the Objection Decision dated 28th July
2020 in respect of the Value Added Tax (VAT) assessment of Kshs
526,022,967.00 and remitted the matter back to the Tribunal for
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 2 OF 34

fresh hearing on the issues of the Corporation Tax and PAYE
assessments.
THE APPEAL
9. The Appellant lodged memorandum of appeal dated 26th August
2020 and filed on 28th September 2020 raising the following
grounds of appeal:
(I) That the Respondent failed to take into account all information
and explanations provided by the Appellant (especially the
Shared Service agreement, Transfer Pricing policy and invoice
copies availed) before arriving at the objection decision;
(II) That the Respondent failed to consider the decision in the
judgment of TAT Case No. l65 of 2017 on the application of
tax exemptions granted to Official Aid Funded Projects (OAFP).
(III) That the Respondent wrongfully assessed income tax on a
project whose income arose from outside Kenya contrary to
Section 4 of the Income Tax Act, CAP 470 of the Laws of Kenya
(hereinafter “ITA”).
(IV) That the Respondent wrongfully charged Value Added Tax
(VAT) on the Merille - Marsabit Road project income contrary
to the decision in the judgment of TAT Case No. 165 of
2017 relating to the same project.
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 3 OF 34

(V) That the Respondent's demand of Kshs 1,955,787,204.00 is
excessive, punitive and beyond the ability of the Appellant to
pay contrary to generally accepted cannons of taxation.
THE APPELLANT’S CASE
10. The Appellant lodged its statement of facts dated 25th August 2020
and filed on 28th September 2020.
11. The Appellant stated that H.P Gauff Ingenieure GmbH & Co KG is
the Kenyan branch of a German multi-sectoral firm of engineering
consultants that has been in operation since 1958. As part of the
Gauff group of companies, the Appellant delivers solutions in a
wide range of professional disciplines. The Gauff group operates
projects with more than 25 branch offices and subsidiaries in Asia,
Europe and Africa in a professional and a customer-oriented way.
Even though all service contracts are entered into by the German
head office, their execution, accounting and tax declaration is
carried out in the respective client countries through locally
registered branches or subsidiaries.
12. It stated that the Respondent conducted an in-depth audit on the
Appellant's tax declarations covering the period 2012 to 2019
years of income and issued its findings and assessments through
letters received on 16th January 2020 and 30th day of March 2020
(but dated 25th March 2020) respectively. In the assessment letter
dated 25th March 2020, the Respondent demanded an additional
tax amounting to Kshs 1,955,787,203.00 which comprised of Kshs
1,360,591,271.00 in principal tax and a total penalty and interest
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 4 OF 34

of Kshs 595,195,932.00. The taxes assessed included Corporation
tax, Pay As You Earn tax ("PAYE") and Value-Added Tax (VAT").
13. The Appellant objected to the entire additional assessment
amounting to Kshs 1,360,591,271.00 in principal tax and a total
penalty and interest of Kshs 595,195,932.00. It stated that the
notice of objection dated 28th April 2020 was received by the
Respondent on 30th April 2020. According to the Appellant, the
Respondent then issued its objection decision on 28th July 2020
(but dated 22nd July 2020) wherein the Respondent retained the
original assessment dated 25th March 2020 and demanded
additional taxes amounting to Kshs 1,955,787,203.00 which
comprised of Kshs 1,360,591,271.00 in principal tax and a total
penalty and interest of Kshs 595,195,932.00. The Appellant then
filed the appeal.
Corporation Tax
14. Regarding under declaration of project revenue in relation to
Official Aid Funded Project- being Merille Marsabit Supervision, the
Appellant stated that the Respondent erroneously concluded in its
objection decision that the Appellant did not meet the threshold
set out under Section 13 of the ITA therefore did not qualify for
exemption of the income from this project. The Appellant stated
that it explained in the objection notice that in the decision under
TAT Case, No. 165 of 2017 validated any tax exemption unless
where it is proved that the taxpayer failed to discharge any of his
duties towards processing of the exemption.
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 5 OF 34

15. The Appellant noted that the Respondent requested for the
Financing agreement which guaranteed the tax exemption which
is a confidential Government document not shared with the
Appellant. The Appellant stated that the principal of legitimate
expectation for Tax exemption is already set out under the
Instructions to Tenderers as ruled under TAT Case No. 165 of
2017.
16. On other Projects' revenue under declared in Kenya, the Appellant
stated that the Respondent had failed to consider the information
and explanations shared in the objection notice, namely: Shared
services agreement, Transfer pricing policy and additional invoices
and supporting documents.
17. With regards to income not declared in Kenya, the Appellant noted
that the Respondent failed to consider the information and
explanations shared in the Shared services agreement and
Transfer pricing policy. Further, the Appellant averred that the
Respondent declared that all income that arose from outside
Kenya were derived in Kenya hence taxable under the ITA hence
not taxable under section 4 of the ITA. The Appellant disagreed
with this treatment and application of the ITA for two reasons:
(i) Taxation of worldwide income – Whereas the Appellant agrees
with the Respondent’s interpretation of Section 3 of the ITA
that provides for taxation of 'worldwide income' accrued by
Kenyan tax residents, the Appellant asserted that the
'worldwide income' (income from Yei water supply project)
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 6 OF 34

under dispute was earned/ accrued by the Appellant and not
its personnel. The Appellant therefore maintained that it was
improper to deem this income as taxable in Kenya by the
mere fact that the personnel involved were Kenyan residents.
It argued that these personnel did not earn the income from
this project hence cannot be deemed to have accrued or
derived it in Kenya (on behalf of the Kenyan branch office).
(ii) Nexus of the income - The taxation of the income of a non-
resident (permanent establishment - PE) is guided by Section
4 of the ITA which only applies tax on income earned from
outside Kenya where it is earned tax by residents only. The
non-resident entities of foreign enterprises operating in Kenya
are only taxed on income accrued or derived from Kenya.
(iii) In relation to Regional Office services, the Appellant stated
that the Respondent failed to consider the information and
explanations shared in the services agreement and Transfer
pricing policy. The Appellant noted that the Respondent
disagrees with classification of these services as 'low value
adding intra group services. According to the Appellant, the
services offered like administrative support are not the core
services of the Appellant as pointed out in the objection
decision. It therefore stated that the provisions under the
2015 OECD BEPS Action 8 to 10 Report on 'low value adding
intra group' services and the standardized mark-up
recommended therein need to be considered.
Value Added Tax
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 7 OF 34

18. With regards to VAT on project revenue not declared in Kenya and
VAT Exemption certificates not availed, the Appellant stated that it
explained in the objection notice that the Yei Water Supply project
is a project where services were provided to a 'recipient outside
Kenya for use, consumption or enjoyment outside Kenya' as per
VAT Regulations 2017 since the project location was in South
Sudan.
19. According to the Appellant, the Respondent requested for
exemption letters contrary to the ruling in the TAT Case No. 165
of 2017. According to the Appellant, the matter under contention
was whether the absence of a VAT exemption letter should render
the supplies in question standard rated. It pointed out that the
ruling set out two main conditions that should stay any
enforcement measures by the Respondent in instances where an
exemption letter is not available. It stated that the conditions are:
(i) It must be proven that the taxpayer discharged all his duties
in the administrative process of obtaining an exemption letter;
and
(ii) The Respondent is unable to present any legal reason for
rejecting a taxpayer's application for the tax exemption.
20. According to the Appellant, the ruling cited the principle of
legitimate expectation as follows:
‘‘legal certainty births legitimate expectation and to levy tax
based on administrative procedure not expressly provided for
and when its quite clear that the Appellant relied on express
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 8 OF 34

provisions would amount to infringing on its legitimate
expectation created by the wording of the law, a legitimate
expectation may arise either from an express promise given
on behalf of a public authority or from the existence of a
regular practice which the claimant can reasonably expect to
continue"
21. In relation to invoices without ETR, the Appellant stated that it
already shared the requested information. In noted that according
to the objection decision, the Respondent stated that some of the
documents presented were not clear. However, the Appellant
stated that the Respondent did not present a list of the specific
documents which it required.
22. In relation to VAT on overtime supervision services, the Appellant
stated that the Respondent failed to consider the decision in the
judgement of the TAT Case No. 165 of 2017 relating to the
same transaction where it was ruled as follows.
"With regard to the contract of Merille-Marsabit, the Tribunal has
reviewed the contract dated June 2012 and agrees with the
Appellant's sentiments that the contract is between the Ministry of
Transport and the Appellant's head office, which is an autonomous
foreign company. Further, the Appellant has demonstrated that
PAYE was paid for the staff engaged under this contract. With the
evidence on record, and without prejudice to the foregoing, we
note that the said project as can be adduced from Clause 2 of the
contract, is funded by the European Union and consequently
falling within the ambit of finance Official Aid Funded Projects, and
VAT is therefore zero-rated'"
Pay As You Earn
23. Regarding low pay for expatriate employees and expatriate
employees not in the payroll, the Appellant stated that the
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 9 OF 34

Respondent failed to consider the following information and
explanations that the Appellant presented in the objection notice:
(i) The benchmarks applied in the workings seem to have only
matched the job titles in the Appellant's employment
contracts and not the duties and tasks mentioned. This greatly
distorts the comparability of your benchmark. It is important
that the benchmarks reflect the duties of personnel in
engineering consultancy business.
(ii) Since the contracts are based on German (tax) law, it is
important to consider emoluments awarded as tax free daily
subsistence allowance. The Daily rate of 42 Euro is included
with 30 days per calendar-months. There is a legitimate
expectation to exempt this from the benchmarked salary since
the foreign expatriates remain tax residents of Germany as
per the Kenya Germany DTA. It is worth mentioning that the
official EU per diem rate is 281€/ day.
(iii) There are no annual bonus payments made as considered in
your workings.
Appellant’s prayers
24. The Appellant prayed for the following reliefs:
(a) That the Objection filed by the Appellant be upheld;
(b) That the Objection Decision be set aside; and
(c) That the Tribunal do issue such other orders deemed
appropriate.
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 10 OF 34

RESPONDENT’S CASE
25. In response to the appeal, the Respondent lodged a Statement of
facts dated and filed on 30th September 2020 wherein it stated
that the Appellant was registered on August 1986 in Kenya under
a certificate of compliance as a branch of H:P Gauff Ingenieure
GMBH & CO (H.P Gauff-JBG). It pointed out that the principal
activity of the Kenya branch is the provision of consultancy and
engineering services mainly in the infrastructure sector.
26. According to the Respondent, the Kenyan branch is headed by
Director, East and southern Africa and is responsible for
overseeing projects in Kenya, Ethiopia, Tanzania, Uganda and
Zambia. Some of its main projects include-
(i) Kisumu-Kakamega road(A1)
(ii) Merille-Marsabit road
(iii) MRTS Jogoo corridor
(iv) Nakuru-Loruk-Marich road
27. The Respondent’s case was that it issued a notice of intention to
audit dated 12th February 2019. The parties held a meeting on 13th
August 2019 to review the preliminary findings. The review was
concluded and the taxpayer issued with a preliminary letter of
findings dated 16th January 2020. The Appellant responded to the
findings letter on 14th February 2020.
28. The Respondent issued a notice of assessment on 25th March
2020. The Appellant then objected vide a notice of objection dated
28th April 2020. The respondent reviewed the objection and issued
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 11 OF 34

an objection decision dated 28th July 2020 confirming the
assessment. The Appellant then lodged this appeal.
29. In response to Appellant’s assertion that the Respondent failed to
take into account the information and explanations provided by
the Appellant especially the shared service agreement, Transfer
Pricing policy, and invoices availed before arriving at the objection
decision, the Respondent contended that this assertion was not
true. The Respondent averred that it fully relied on all the
documents that the Appellant provided and made reference to
these documents in the objection decision.
30. In response to the assertion that the Respondent failed to consider
the decision in the judgement in TAT Appeal No.165 of 2017 HP
Gauff Ingenieure GMBH & Co K.G v Commissioner of Domestic
Taxes on the application of tax exemptions granted to official Aid
Funded projects (OAFP), the Respondent stated that the Tribunal
Judgement in TAT Appeal No.165 of 2017 delivered on 30th
March 2020 determined that KRA stay the tax demanded to allow
the Appellant (H.P Gauff) 120 days to obtain exemption
certificates. According to the Respondent, the timelines allowed by
this Honourable Tribunal have elapsed and there has been no
indication by the Appellant that it will avail the exemption
certificates as per the orders of the Tribunal.
31. In response to the assertion that the Respondent wrongfully
assessed income tax on a project whose incomes arose outside
Kenya contrary to Section 4 of the ITA, the Respondent stated that
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 12 OF 34

the Appellant's assertion that the projects incomes should not be
charged to tax in Kenya is not correct for the following reasons:
(a) Under declaration of Project Revenue for Official Aid Funded
Project (OAFP)- Merille Marsabit Road Supervision.
32. The Appellant claimed that the project is exempt from tax based
on the instructions in the Financing agreement KE/FED/2009/021-
655 between Kenya and the European Union that the Respondent
was not privy to.
33. Section 13 of the ITA provides for exemption of certain incomes
from income tax and that the Appellant did not meet the threshold
of section 13 of the ITA and therefore, these incomes did not
qualify as exempt incomes and were therefore rightly brought to
charge.
(b) Under declaration of Project Revenue for Projects amounting to
Kshs.787,622,509.00
34. According to the Respondent, the Appellant shared service
agreement and the transfer pricing policy. The service agreement
was to support the costs wholly and exclusively incurred by H.P
Gauff Head office and which necessitated the invoicing of some
projects directly by Head office in Euros as well as to demonstrate
criteria for recharging within the Gauff group.
35. The Respondent stated that the Appellant provided the Transfer
Pricing Policy to support the recharge of costs to the Kenyan
branch by its German head office. The Transfer pricing policy
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 13 OF 34

included a benchmarking analysis report that guides on an
appropriate mark-up. From the Respondent's analysis and
understanding of the H.P Gauff business, the contracts entered
into related majorly to provision of supervision services which are
carried out in Kenya. The Respondent argued that the personnel
engaged in these projects are employees of the Appellant.
36. The Respondent therefore, asserted that it was right in bringing to
charge income from the three projects in accordance with Section
3(2)(a)(i) and Section 15 of the ITA.
(c) Income not declared in Kenya
37. The Respondent pointed out that the Appellant entered into
contracts but did not report these incomes in Kenya. It contented
that these incomes were incomes derived in Kenya and therefore
should be charged in Kenya as per the provisions of Section 3(1) of
the ITA.
(d) Regional Office Services
38. According to the Respondent, the Appellant in the objection stated
that the Respondent's benchmarking analysis was incomparable
and failed to consider recent developments in BEPS Action reports.
The Respondent also noted that the Appellant also highlighted
provisions under the 2015 OECD BEPS Action 8 to 10 report on
"low value adding intra group services and the standardized mark-
up recommendation.”
39. The Respondent noted that these are not low value services as
they constitute the core business of the group. Further, the mere
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 14 OF 34

reimbursement of costs is not at arm's length and therefore the
Respondent's benchmarking analysis adopted to arrive at an arm's
length remuneration was correct as it sampled comparables that
are similar in functions performed, assets employed and risks
faced. The Respondent asserted that these Incomes were incomes
derived in Kenya and therefore, should be charged in Kenya as per
the provisions of Section 3(1) of the ITA.
40. The Respondent noted that subsequent to the review, the
Appellant has been declaring and paying taxes on these incomes
in Kenya.
41. In response to the Appellant’s assertion that the Respondent
wrongfully charged VAT on the Merille Marsabit Road project
income contrary to the decision in the judgement of TAT Appeal
No.165 of 2017 HP Gauff Ingenieure GMBH & Co K.G v
Commissioner of Domestic Taxes, the Respondent maintained
that this was not true for the following reasons:
(i) On VAT on project Revenue not declared in Kenya, the
Appellant did not provide any document that is, exemption
certificates and therefore VAT remains due and payable.
(ii) On invoices without ETR, the Appellant provided invoices
without ETR receipts thus were disallowed. Invoices without
ETR receipts cannot be allowed for Input VAT purposes as
provided by Section 17 of the Value Added Tax Act CAP 476 of
the Laws of Kenya (hereinafter “VATA”).
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 15 OF 34

(iii) In relation to VAT on overtime supervision services, the
Appellant provided supervision services outside of the normal
working hours to Gulsan Insaat Sanayi Turizm - Kenya branch
that was the main contractors in the construction of Merille-
Marsabit Road and to China Overseas Engineering Group who
were the main contractors in the construction of Kisumu-
Kakamega Road. The Respondent relied on the VATA which
defines a service as:
'anything that is not goods or money' while supply of services
has been defined as; "anything done that is not a supply of
goods or money, including the performance of services for
another person; the grant, assignment, or surrender of any
right; the making available of any facility or advantage; or the
toleration of any situation or the refraining from the doing of
any act.’
(iv) The Respondent contended that it is not in dispute that the
performance of services outside of the normal working hours
constitute supply of services. It noted further that the VATA
defines a taxable supply as "a supply, other than an exempt
supply, made in Kenya by a person in the course or
furtherance of a business carried on by the person.". The
Respondent therefore asserted that the provision of services
by the Appellant outside of the normal working hours
constitutes a service provided in the course of its business
hence the services were vatable.
(v) In relation to VAT Exemption certificates not availed-Official
aid funded projects (Kisumu-Kakamega road), the Respondent
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 16 OF 34

cited Paragraph 20 of Part II of the First schedule to the VATA
which provides for the supply of the services that are be
exempt supplies.
42. According to the Respondent, the National Treasury on 21st
December 2010 issued a circular Ref DFN 415/232/011 which
provided for procedures for exemption from duties and VAT for
official Aid funded projects. The Respondent pointed out that the
guidelines outlined by treasury for exemption from duties are as
follows:
(i) The sector Ministry is required to make an application to the
permanent secretary/Treasury confirming that under the
particular project, there will be contracts for the provision of
services such as consultancies, advertisements, workshops
etc.
(ii) On receipt of such confirmation, the treasury will write to the
Kenya Revenue Authority to confirm that the particular project
is an official aid funded project and services to the project
qualify for zero-rating.
(iii) The letter will be copied to the sector ministry which is
expected to forward any request for zero-rating under the
project to the Respondent for the necessary processing
without coming back to the treasury; and
(iv) The request to the Respondent for zero-rating should be made
before the service is provided based on the quotation and
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 17 OF 34

proforma invoices provided by the service provider. This will
enable the Respondent to authorize the service provider to do
so on zero rated basis but on a case by case basis.
43. The Respondent argued that in as much as there was evidence
that the project was an official aid funded project, the Appellant
has not yet submitted to the Respondent any exemption letters
from the National Treasury. The Respondent also pointed out that
the only authority that can be relied on is the explicit instructions
from the National Treasury to treat services, goods or equipment
as exempt detailing the specific proforma invoices to which the
exemption applies.
44. It asserted that the judgment delivered on 30th March 2020 in TAT
Appeal No.165 of 2017 HP Gauff Ingenieure GMBH & Co K.G
v Commissioner of Domestic Taxes directed the Respondent to
stay the tax demanded to give the Appellant 120 days to obtain
exemption certificates. The Respondent asserted that there has
been no indication by the Appellant that it will avail the said
exemption certificates.
45. The Respondent maintained that the VAT amounts remain payable
as demanded until the Appellant furnishes the Respondent with
the exemption certificates.
46. In response to assertion that the Respondent's demand of Kshs.1,
955,787,204.00 was excessive, punitive and beyond the ability of
the appellant to pay contrary to generally accepted canons of
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 18 OF 34

taxation, the Respondent stated that through the audit process, it
engaged the Appellant as evidenced by correspondence. The
Respondent maintained that it was not true that the tax demanded
is excessive and punitive as alleged by the Appellant. The
Respondent maintained that its statutory obligation is enshrined
under Article 210(1) of the Constitution of Kenya, 2010
(hereinafter “the Constitution”) to assess and collect taxes on
behalf of the Government of Kenya.
47. The Respondent stated that mandate as provided under Section 5
of the Act is inter alia to administer written laws listed in the first
schedule and to assess, collect and account for all revenues in
accordance with those laws.
48. It asserted that if in execution of its mandate, a person ought to be
taxed comes within the letter of the law, then the person must be
taxed.
49. The Respondent pointed out that the issues of alleged motives will
not matter in place of clear provisions of the law. In this regard,
the Respondent urged the Tribunal to consider the case of
Republic v Commissioner of Domestic Taxes Large Tax
paper's Office Exparte Barclays Bank of Kenya Limited
[2012] eKLR.
Respondent’s prayers
50. In light of the foregoing, the Respondent prayed as follows:
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 19 OF 34

(a) The Tribunal do uphold the Respondent's decision to charge
tax amounting to Kshs, 1,955,787,204.00 inclusive of
penalties and interest; and
(b) The Appeal be dismissed with costs to the Respondent.
PARTIES WRITTEN SUBMISSIONS
Appellant’s written submissions
51. The Appellant filed written submissions dated 8th August 2023 and
filed on 11th August 2023. It submitted that the Respondent did not
consider all information and explanations provided by the
Appellant especially the Shared Service agreement, Transfer
Pricing policy and invoice copies before arriving at the objection
decision.
52. The Appellant submitted that the Respondent overlooked the
decision in the judgment of TAT Case No.165 of2017 on the
application of tax exemptions granted to Official Aid Funded
Projects.
53. It submitted that the Respondent wrongfully assessed income tax
on a project whose income arose from outside Kenya contrary to
Section 4 of the ITA.
Respondent’s Written Submission
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 20 OF 34

54. The Respondent submitted that it considered all the information
and explanations provided by the Appellant before arriving at the
objection decision.
55. On whether the subject projects were exempt from VAT, the
Respondent submitted that the Appellant did not provide any
document that is, exemption certificates therefore, the projects
were taxable.
56. On whether the income form the subject projects was derived from
or outside Kenya, it submitted that the incomes were derived in
Kenya and therefore should be charged in Kenya as per the
provisions of Section 3(1) of the ITA.
57. On whether the Respondent's assessment is excessive, punitive
and beyond the ability of the appellant to pay contrary to
generally accepted canons of taxation, the Respondent submitted
that it relied on information and documents provided by the
Appellant.
58. It submitted that if in execution of its mandate, a person ought to
be taxed comes within the letter of the law, then he must be taxed
and that the issues of alleged motives will not matter in place of
clear provisions of the law. It cited the case of Republic vs.
Commissioner of Domestic Taxes Large Tax paper's Office
Experte Barclays Bank of Kenya Limited [2012] eKLR to
support this position.
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 21 OF 34

59. The Respondent cited the cases of Sheria Sacco Society
Limited v Commissioner of Domestic Taxes [2019] eKLR,
and Tumaini Distributors Company (K) Limited v
Commissioner of Domestic Taxes [2020] eKLR to submit that
the Appellant has a duty to prove the assessment was incorrect
but the Appellant failed to discharged the burden.
ISSUES FOR DETERMINATION
60. Upon consideration by the Tribunal of the decision by the High
Court in appeal number 078 of 2024 the following have been
identified by the Tribunal as the two issues for its determination:
(a) Whether the Respondent erred in confirming corporation
tax.
(b) Whether the Respondent erred in confirming PAYE
assessment.
ANALYSIS AND FINDINGS
(a) Whether the Respondent erred in confirming corporation
tax
Merille Marsabit Supervision
61. The Appellant raised a number of issues in relation to corporation
tax. It asserted that Merille Marsabit Supervision was an Official
Aid Funded Project. It argued that the decision in the ruling under
TAT Case, No. 165 of 2017 validated that any tax exemption
unless where it is proved that the taxpayer failed to discharge any
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 22 OF 34

of its duties towards processing of the exemption. It also noted
that the Respondent request for the financing agreement which
guaranteed the tax exemption was a confidential Government
document not shared with the Appellant.
62. On the other hand, the Respondent argued that the Appellant
failed to demonstrate that it qualified for tax exemption under
Section 13 of the ITA.
63. Section 13 of the ITA provides for certain income that is exempt
from tax. It provides as follows:
“13. Certain income exempt from tax, etc.
(1) Notwithstanding anything in Part II, the income specified in
Part I of the First Schedule which accrued in or was derived
from Kenya shall be exempt from tax to the extent so
specified.
(2) The Minister may, by notice in the Gazette, provide—
(a) that any income or class of income which accrued in or
was derived from Kenya shall be exempt from tax to the
extent specified in such notice;
(b) that any exemption under subsection (1) of this section
shall cease to have effect either generally or to the extent
specified in the notice.
(3) A notice under subsection (2) of this section shall be laid
before the National Assembly without unreasonable delay, and
if a resolution is passed by the Assembly within twenty days
on which it next sits after the notice is so laid that the notice
be annulled, it shall thenceforth be void, but without prejudice
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 23 OF 34

to the validity of anything previously done thereunder, or to
the issuing of a new notice…”
64. Whereas the First Schedule to ITA provides for a list of incomes
that are exempt from tax to the extent so specified, the Appellant
did not single out the provisions under the first schedule that
exempts its income from taxation. Secondly, the Appellant did not
provide a Gazette notice issued under Section 13(2) of the ITA
granting exemptions to the Appellant.
65. We note that whereas it appears that the Appellant entered into
contracts, it did not file those contracts to support its case. The
Appellant also did not file invoices to support its claim.
66. It appears that the Appellant placed blanket reliance on the finding
in TAT Case No. 165 of 2017. We wish to pint out two things
about that case. First, that case dealt with VAT. It did not deal with
income tax as it was not in issue. Secondly, the exemptions under
VAT regime are not the same as those under ITA and cannot be
applied interchangeably. The Appellant cannot therefore, assert
that since the finding in TAT Case No. 165 of 2017 which found
that VAT was exempt also applies to exemptions under ITA.
67. Be that as it may, the Appellant did not provide documentary
evidence to support this issue. It appears that the Appellant
sought the Tribunal to rely on TAT Case No. 165 of 2017 to
determine this case which is not the correct position.
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 24 OF 34

68. Based on the foregoing, the Tribunal finds that the Appellant failed
to demonstrate that corporation tax under the project was exempt
from taxation.
Income not declared in Kenya
69. The Appellant asserted that the Respondent failed to consider the
information and explanations shared on the Shared services
agreement and Transfer pricing policy and that the Respondent
declared that all income that arose from outside Kenya were
derived in Kenya hence taxable under the ITA. At this junction, we
wish to point out that the Appellant did not file Shared Services
Agreement and Transfer Pricing Policy therefore, the Tribunal
could not verify whether the Appellant’s statement was factual or
not.
70. Whereas the Appellant asserted that the income from Yei water
supply project under dispute was earned accrued by the Appellant
and not its personnel, there was no evidence to support that
assertion.
71. The Appellant also stated that taxation of the income of a non-
resident (permanent establishment - PE) is guided by Section 4 of
the ITA which only applies tax on income earned from outside
Kenya where it is earned tax residents only. The non-resident
entities (PEs) of foreign enterprises operating in Kenya are only
taxed on income accrued or derived from Kenya.
72. In its objection dated 28th April 2020, the Appellant stated that Yei
water supply project was located in South Sudan. It stated that
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 25 OF 34

income from Yei water supply was earned by Kenyan branch of
Gauff entity (Kenyan non-resident) and not its personnel. The
Appellant argued that it was improper to deem the income as
taxable in Kenya by mere virtue that the personnel were Kenyan
residents.
73. The Respondent asserted that incomes from the Appellant’s
projects were incomes derived in Kenya and therefore should be
charged in Kenya as per the provisions of section 3(1) of the ITA.
74. Section 4 (a) of the ITA provides as follows:
“4. Income from businesses
For the purposes of section 3(2)(a)(i)—
(a) where a business is carried on or exercised partly within
and partly outside Kenya by a resident person, the whole
of the gains or profits from such business shall be deemed to
have accrued in or to have been derived from Kenya. (emphasis
is ours)”
75. The question then is who is a resident person for purposes of
income tax, and whether the Appellant and/or its personnel were
residents for purposes of income tax. In this regard, Section 2(1) of
the ITA defines ‘‘resident’’ as follows:
"resident", when applied in relation—
(a) to an individual, means—
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 26 OF 34

(i) that he has a permanent home in Kenya and was present in
Kenya for any period in any particular year of income under
consideration; or
(ii) that he has no permanent home in Kenya but—
(A) was present in Kenya for a period or periods amounting
in the aggregate to 183 days or more in that year of
income; or
(B) was present in Kenya in that year of income and in each of
the two preceding years of income for periods averaging
more than 122 days in each year of income;
(b) to a body of persons, means—
(i) that the body is a company incorporated under a law of
Kenya;
Or
(ii) that the management and control of the affairs of the body
was exercised in Kenya in a particular year of income under
consideration; or
(iii) that the body has been declared by the Minister, by notice
in the Gazette, to be resident in Kenya for any year of
income.”
76. We note that the residence of the ‘personnel’ is not disputed.
However, the Appellant did not define the composition of these
‘personnel’ as to whether they were directors, managers who have
control and management over a company, or subordinate staff
who do not have management or control over the Appellant. The
directors and managers play a key role in management of a
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 27 OF 34

company and play a key role under management and control
principle when determining effective control of the entity that is
being taxed. Be that as it may, the Appellant conceded that the
personnel were residents.
77. The Appellant’s case was that income from Yei water supply was
earned by Kenyan branch of Gauff entity (Kenyan non-resident)
and not its personnel. It argued that it was improper to deem the
income as taxable in Kenya by mere virtue that the personnel
were Kenyan residents. In other words, the Appellant’s case was
that the income from South Sudan was not taxable in Kenya on
basis that that the Appellant was not non-resident in Kenya.
78. However, the Appellant at paragraph 1 of its statement of facts
stated as follows:
‘‘1. H.P Gauff Ingenieure GmbH & Co KG (herein referred to as
"the Appellant") is the Kenyan branch of a German multi-
sectoral firm of engineering consultants that has been in
operation since 1958.’’
79. Under Companies Act Cap 486 of the Laws of Kenya, a company
cannot be a branch unless it is registered in Kenya. The Appellant
admitted that it is a Kenyan branch and further admitted that its
income is accrued in or derived and accordingly it is a resident for
tax purposes as defined under Section 2(1) of the ITA.
80. The Tribunal notes that the Appellant did not define the
composition of these ‘personnel’. We have also pointed out above
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 28 OF 34

that the Appellant admitted that the ‘personnel’ were Kenyan
residents. Consequently, we find that under the management and
control principle as provided for under Section 2(1) article (ii) of
the ITA, the management and control of the Appellant happened
from Kenya. As such, Yei water supply project located in South
Sudan was managed from Kenya and the income was derived from
Kenya by the Appellant.
81. Consequently, pursuant to section 4(a) of the ITA the Appellant
operated Yei water supply from Kenya and under section 4(a) of
the ITA, such income is taxable in Kenya.
82. In the locus classicus case of De Beers Consolidated Mines,
Ltd v Howe (Surveyor of Taxes), [1906] A.C. 455 the House
of Lords established the principle that a company’s tax residence
is based on the location where its central management and control
is exercised rather than merely its place of incorporation or
primary operations.
83. In the present appeal, we have established that the Appellant is
not only incorporated in Kenya, but it exercised management and
control while in Kenya therefore, the Respondent was right that
the income was taxable in Kenya.
Regional Office services
84. On this issue, the Appellant stated that the Respondent failed to
consider the information and explanations shared in the Shared
services agreement and Transfer pricing policy. The Appellant also
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 29 OF 34

stated that the Respondent disagreed with classification of these
services as 'low value adding intra group services. The Tribunal
has already pointed out above that the Appellant did not provide
Shared services agreement and Transfer pricing policy even
through it claims that it presented them to the Respondent.
85. Whereas the Appellant contended that it adduced documents
before the Respondent, it did not file those documents to support
this appeal. We reiterate that the duty to demonstrate that the
Respondent’s decision is incorrect under section 56(1) of the TPA
does not terminate at objection stage. The obligations continue
when the taxpayer files an appeal before this Tribunal. Section 30
of the Tax Appeals Tribunal Act Cap 469A( hereinafter “TATA”)
provides as follows:
“In a proceeding before the Tribunal, the appellant has the
burden of proving—
(a) Where an appeal relates to an assessment, that the
assessment is excessive; or
(b) In any other case, that the tax decision should not have
been made or should have been made differently.”
86. In the case of Singapore Motors Limited v Commissioner of
Domestic Taxes (Income Tax Appeal E039 of 2021) [2024]
KEHC 2443 (KLR), the High Court held as follows:
‘‘This Court has remained emphatic that under section 30 of
the Tax Appeals Tribunal Act (TATA) and section 56 of the Tax
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 30 OF 34

Procedures Act (TPA), the burden of proving that an
assessment is wrong or excessive remains upon the
taxpayer.’’
87. The Tribunal can only fault the Respondent when the Appellant
tables the documents that it placed before the Respondent. That is
how we can determine whether the Respondent erred.
88. Consequently, we find and hold that the Appellant failed to
demonstrate that the Respondent erred in confirming corporation
tax.
(b) Whether the Respondent erred in confirming PAYE
assessment
89. The Appellant asserted that the benchmarks applied by the
Respondent in its workings seem to have only matched the job
titles in the Appellant's employment contracts and not the duties
and tasks mentioned which according to the Appellant greatly
distorted the comparability of the benchmark. It asserted that it
was important that the benchmarks reflect the duties of personnel
in engineering consultancy business. The Appellant also
maintained that emoluments awarded should have been tax free
as they were daily subsistence allowance. It asserted that it had a
legitimate expectation to exempt emoluments from the
benchmarked salary since the foreign expatriates remain tax
residents of Germany as per the Kenya Germany DTA.
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 31 OF 34

90. On the other hand, the Respondent submitted that it noted that
some remuneration of some expatriates was low compared to the
locals, that the Appellant did not table employment contracts and
that some expatriates’ salaries were not subjected to PAYE as they
were omitted from payroll.
91. The Tribunal notes that the Appellant did not provide any
documentary evidence to prove its case. Not a contract of
employment, nor payroll.
92. Whereas the Appellant argued that its foreign expatriates remain
tax residents of Germany pursuant to the the Kenya Germany
DTA, the Appellant did not provide any proof that the expatriates
were German. We note that the Kenya Germany DTA applies to
the nationals of the two countries. In particular, Article 3(1)(f) of
the DTA provides as follows:
“(1) In this Agreement, unless the context otherwise requires:
—
(f) the term "nationals" means:
(i) in respect of the Federal Republic of Germany—
all Germans in the meaning of paragraph 1 of Article 116 of
the Basic Law for the Federal Republic of Germany and all
legal persons, partnerships and associations deriving their
status as such from the law in force in the Federal Republic of
Germany;
JUDGEMENT- TAT NO. E442-2020 H.P GAUFF INGENIEURE GMBH & CO KG V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
PAGE 32 OF 34

(ii) in respect of Kenya— all individuals possessing the
nationality of Kenya and all legal persons, partnerships and
associations deriving their status as such from the law in force
in
Kenya;”
93. Whereas the Appellant sought refuge under the Kenya Germany
DTA, it failed to prove that the expatriates were German.
94. Based on the foregoing, the Tribunal finds that the Appellant failed
to demonstrated that the Respondent erred in confirming PAYE
assessment.
FINAL DECISION
95. The upshot to the foregoing is that the Tribunal finds and holds
that the Appeal is devoid of merit and makes the following orders:
(a) The Appeal be and is hereby dismissed
(b) The Respondent’s confirmation of the Corporation tax and
PAYE as outlined in its Objection Decision dated 22nd July 2020
be and is hereby upheld.
(c) Each party to bear its own cost.
96. It is so Ordered.
DATED and DELIVERED at NAIROBI this 26th day of March, 2026.
